project.name: evm-agent-skills
project.summary: A deep operational skill package for AI agents building production-grade EVM protocols, starting with Solidity and Foundry testing methodology.
non_goals: Do not build a generic web3 link directory.
Do not scaffold toy ERC20 projects.
Do not claim to automatically audit contracts in v0.
Do not compete with Foundry's compiler, test runner, fuzzing engine, or deploy tooling.
Do not make skills into essays without executable agent workflows.

positioning.core_thesis: Foundry provides the EVM development substrate. evm-agent-skills provides the agent-readable methodology layer: how to structure Solidity, derive tests, unify deploy/test setup, bound fuzzing, and use deterministic Foundry feedback safely.
value_beyond_foundry: Operational checklists and examples for production-grade Solidity workflows.
Exact pinned references to audited or production-proven implementations.
Skills that can be loaded narrowly per task instead of one giant context dump.
A future dependency for agentic-solidity CLI and other agent runners.
positioning.quality_bar: If a skill could fit in a basic Ethereum cheat-sheet, it is too shallow for this repo. Each skill must help an agent make better engineering decisions and produce verifiable code.

audience: AI coding agents working inside EVM protocol repositories
Solidity developers who want repeatable agent workflows
Protocol teams adopting agent-assisted development without lowering safety standards

skill_families.purpose: Teach agents how to structure and write Solidity modules without relying on training-data averages.
Teach agents how to design rigorous Foundry test suites that create deterministic feedback loops.

skill_format.standard: Anthropic-style SKILL.md with optional references, examples, and scripts directories.
requirements: Each skill starts with YAML frontmatter containing name and description.
Each skill explains when to use it and when not to use it.
Each skill contains a step-by-step agent workflow.
Each skill includes concrete Foundry/Solidity examples where useful.
Each skill links exact source files, preferably pinned to commit SHAs.
Each skill includes verification commands and expected outcomes.
Each skill avoids vague advice and subjective claims without checks.
anti_patterns: Security claims without an explicit review pass.
Copying large external codebases into the repo.
Generic Ethereum basics better suited for ethskills.

criteria: Clear explanation of why the reference is included
initial_candidates: OpenZeppelin for primitives and standard patterns
Sablier for streaming and advanced testing references
Hifi V3 for branching-tree and testing methodology references

milestones.title: Repository foundation
milestones.goal: Create the repo skeleton, manifest, docs, and authoring standards.
deliverables: skills/manifest.json exists and validates as JSON
Empty skill directories exist for v0 scope

milestones.title: Deploy/test unification skill
milestones.goal: Create the first flagship skill showing how Foundry deploy scripts and test setup should share construction logic.
deliverables: At least one reference markdown file
Minimal Foundry example demonstrating shared deploy logic
Verification commands using forge build and forge test

milestones.title: Branching-tree testing skill
milestones.goal: Document the branch-complete spec workflow that maps .tree specs into concrete and fuzz tests.
deliverables: Agent workflow for deriving tests from a tree

milestones.title: Fuzz and invariant skills
milestones.goal: Teach agents how to derive useful fuzz bounds and build invariant handler/store patterns.
deliverables: Examples showing useful vs useless fuzz domains

milestones.title: Write-solidity skill family
milestones.goal: Create initial code-structure and style skills that guide contract architecture before tests are written.

milestones.title: Package readiness for CLI consumption
milestones.goal: Make the skill package easy for a future CLI to resolve, pin, and load narrowly per task.
deliverables: Manifest lists all skills with ids, paths, descriptions, tags, and version
README documents how another tool should consume the package
Basic validation script or documented validation command

tasks.title: Create initial repository foundation
acceptance: README explains the repo in plain language
prd.json validates as JSON
manifest exists and validates as JSON
docs explain reference curation and skill authoring rules

tasks.title: Create v0 skill directory skeletons
acceptance: All v0 skill directories exist
Each skill directory contains SKILL.md placeholder with correct frontmatter
References directories exist where needed

tasks.title: Author deploy/test unification skill
acceptance: Skill explains why test setup must use deployment logic
Skill includes Foundry layout and copyable patterns
Example passes forge build and forge test
References are exact and preferably pinned

tasks.title: Author branching-tree technique skill
acceptance: Skill defines .tree spec structure
Skill maps tree branches to concrete tests
Skill maps constraints to fuzz bounds
Example includes contract, tree, and tests

tasks.title: Author fuzz bounds and invariant skills
acceptance: Fuzz skill shows constraint-derived bound selection
Invariant skill shows handler/store pattern
Both include verification commands and anti-patterns

tasks.title: Author initial write-solidity skills
acceptance: Skills are operational and agent-facing
Skills include concrete Solidity examples
Skills avoid generic beginner material

tasks.title: Validate package for future CLI consumption
acceptance: Manifest has stable ids and paths
Every manifest skill path exists
README documents package consumption model
No skill is referenced vaguely by repo root only

definition_of_done_v0: The repo contains a valid manifest and stable skill paths.
At least four deep v0 skills are authored: deploy/test unification, branching-tree technique, fuzz bounds, invariant handlers.
At least one write-solidity skill exists for module structure.
At least one Foundry example compiles and tests successfully.
All external references are exact links, preferably pinned to commit SHAs.
The package can be consumed by a future CLI without hard-coding skill content.
open_questions: Should this package stay EVM-only or eventually include broader onchain skills?
Which exact Hifi V3 files should be the first pinned references?
Should examples be full Foundry projects or minimal snippets until the CLI exists?
Should skill IDs use slash paths like test-verify/deploy-test-unification or package-style ids like test_verify.deploy_test_unification?
What validation script should enforce manifest-to-path consistency?
